title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
content: Eventually, Biogen completed 2 phase 3 clinical trials with identical designs. Known individually as EMERGE and ENGAGE, both trials were initially terminated on grounds of futility before they were reinstated after additional blinded data became available. Based on this additional data, the researchers determined that EMERGE had met its primary outcome measure (the CDR "sum of boxes"); ENGAGE did not meet its primary outcome, but "benefits were observed" in study participants on higher doses for more extended periods. Knopman et al analyzed the data from 2 clinical trials with aducanumab. The investigators concluded that the efficacy of aducanumab cannot be proven when the 2 clinical trials yield different results. [8]

title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
content: In November 2020, the FDA's Peripheral and Central Nervous System Drugs Advisory Committee met to discuss the aducanumab BLA. A series of 4 votes culminated in the near-unanimous (there was a single abstention) verdict that the advisors did not find reasonable grounds to consider the results of the clinical trials as evidence of aducanumab's effectiveness for treating AD. Within days of the July 2021 accelerated approval decision, 3 members of the Advisory Committee, comprising one-third of its nongovernment members, resigned, citing "serious potential to impair future research" and deeming the FDA's ruling "the worst approval decision that FDA has made....." [9]

title: Emicizumab -- Indications
content: Emicizumab was created to circumvent challenges associated with frequent intravenous administration of FVIII and offer a standardized treatment option for Hemophilia A patients with and without inhibitors. [4] Initially, Emicizumab was first approved by the FDA in 2017 for patients with congenital hemophilia A (in combination with inhibitors). Emcizicumab was subsequently approved for prophylaxis to prevent or reduce the frequency of bleeding episodes in patients with hemophilia A (congenital factor VIII deficiency) with or without factor VIII inhibitors. [5] Emicizumab was also granted priority review and breakthrough therapy designation by FDA. [6]

title: Luspatercept -- Indications -- FDA-Approved Indications
content: Luspatercept is an erythroid maturation agent approved by the FDA for treating anemia in patients with β-thalassemia who need regular red blood cell transfusions. [1] The recommended dose of luspatercept for patients with β-thalassemia is 1 mg/kg subcutaneously once every 3 weeks. [2] [3] The FDA approved this dosage based on the results of the phase III BELIEVE trial. [4] This medication promotes late-stage erythropoiesis, even in patients with β-thalassemia ineffectively controlled with erythropoiesis-stimulating agents (ESA).

title: Evinacumab -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
content: Evinacumab does require prior authorization before administration, with specific guidelines needing to be met, which may vary per insurance company. Another critical factor to consider before the administration of evinacumab is the cost. [19] Due to the novelty of the medication, the expense of evinacumab may be quite high, with the annual wholesale cost as high as $450,000. Citing the substantial price of $450,000 per person per year for evinacumab, the National Lipid Association emphasizes the urgency for cost-effective approaches by proposing a novel technique: customizing doses using weight bands, a departure from the conventional mg/kg method and dose individualization as per the LDL-C response. This method can accomplish a 34% reduction in cost without compromising efficacy and offers a promising strategy to reduce the financial burden. [20]

title: Best Practices For Administering Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus (COVID-19) (Archived) -- Introduction
content: In the race to decrease the global burden of COVID-19, several monoclonal antibodies were developed and granted emergency use authorizations (EUAs). However, as COVID-19 variants emerged, most of the monoclonal antibodies had their EUAs revoked due to limited efficacy against dominant circulating variants and subvariants. This activity reviews the pathophysiology and function of these monoclonal antibodies and the risks and benefits of these agents. However, it must be noted that according to the National Institutes of Health (NIH), none of these monoclonal antibodies are recommended in 2023 for the treatment of COVID-19. The only monoclonal antibody with an active EUA is tixagevimab co-packaged with cilgavimab, which is authorized for preexposure prophylaxis of COVID-19. [9] According to United States Food and Drug Administration (FDA) update on 1/6/2023, a SARS-CoV-2 Omicron subvariant is not anticipated to be neutralized by this monoclonal antibody combination; however, they are waiting for additional data to make their final decision.

title: Bamlanivimab -- Indications
content: Bamlanivimab is a monoclonal antibody (mAb) directed against the spike protein (S-Protein) of severe acute respiratory syndrome-coronavirus-2 (SARS-CoV-2). Bamlanivimab, or LY-COV555, was derived from the convalescent plasma of patients with coronavirus disease 2019 (COVID-19). [1] It is 1 of the potent neutralizing antibodies approved by the Food and Drug Administration (FDA) under Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) in August 2021 to be used in combination with etesivimab (another mAb against S-protein of SARS-CoV-2). [2] The other neutralizing antibodies available are casirivimab and imdevimab, used in combination therapy. [3] [4] [5] The other novel agent in this class is sotrovimab. title: Imdevimab -- Indications
content: On June 3, 2021, the FDA updated the emergency use authorization (EUA), lowering the dosage of casirivimab and imdevimab to 600 mg each, an option for subcutaneous (SQ) administration. Another update on November 17, 2021, authorizing casirivimab and imdevimab (subcutaneously) for emergency use as post-exposure prophylaxis for COVID-19 in adults. It was recommended for patients who are vaccinated but not expected to mount adequate immune response or who are not fully vaccinated. [5] It is recommended that the medication be started as soon as possible after a positive SARS-CoV-2 test and within 10 days of symptom onset. This is because active viral replication occurs between days 1 to 10 of symptoms, and these medications are unlikely to be beneficial later. Monoclonal antibodies are likely associated with worse clinical outcomes when administered to hospitalized COVID-19 patients requiring high-flow oxygen or ventilator support. The imdevimab and casirivimab combination is not authorized in use for patients hospitalized or who require oxygen therapy for COVID-19. Viral load reductions with intravenous (IV) and SQ appear similar, but data on SQ administration is limited. [5] [6]

title: Aducanumab -- Indications -- FDA Conditional Approval Initially Granted in July 2021 under the Program for Accelerated Approval
content: In July 2021, the FDA granted accelerated approval for aducanumab; the decision was not based on the clinical data but rather on an intermediate outcome, namely the drug's ability to reduce the burden of excess β-amyloid in the brain and the attendant "reasonable" likelihood that amyloid reduction would benefit patients. [10] In conjunction with the approval, the FDA required that Biogen conduct a phase 4 clinical trial through 2030 to evaluate risks and benefits over the long term. Enrollment in the phase 4 trial was not a requirement for prescribing aducanumab.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 1 -- Community 174
content: The community defines “DRUGS USED IN DIABETES” as comprising two major super‑classes: **INSULINS AND ANALOGUES** and **BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS, EXCL. INSULINS**. Within INSULINS AND ANALOGUES, the subclass **INSULINS AND ANALOGUES FOR INJECTION, LONG‑ACTING** includes agents such as **INSULIN GLARGINE**, the combination product **INSULIN DEGLUDEC/LIRAGLUTIDE** (which incorporates **LIRAGLUTIDE**), and **INSULIN GLARGINE/LIXISENATIDE** (which incorporates **LIXISENATIDE**). Non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents are organized into several pharmacologic classes. **ALPHA GLUCOSIDASE INHIBITORS** lower blood glucose by delaying carbohydrate absorption. **DIPEPTIDYL PEPTIDASE‑4 (DPP‑4) INHIBITORS**—including **ALOGLIPTIN** and **SITAGLIPTIN** (the latter also listed as a DPP‑4 inhibitor)—are classified under BLOOD GLUCOSE‑LOWERING DRUGS, EXCL. INSULINS. **GLUCAGON‑LIKE PEPTIDE‑1 (GLP‑1) ANALOGUES** comprise **EXENATID**, **LIRAGLUTIDE**, and **LIXISENATIDE**. **BIGUANIDE BLOOD GLUCOSE LOWER DRUGS** represent another subclass; **FENFORMIN (metformin)** is a member, illustrating that biguanides are a subset of the broader non‑insulin glucose‑lowering category. Combination formulations link agents from different classes, exemplified by **SIMVASTATIN/SITAGLIPTIN**, which pairs the statin **SIMVASTATIN** with the DPP‑4 inhibitor **SITAGLIPTIN**. An important drug‑interaction contraindication is noted: **NELFINAVIR MESYLATE** is contraindicated when used with **SIMVASTATIN**. Overall, the taxonomy clarifies that insulin preparations (including long‑acting analogues) and various oral or injectable non‑insulin agents (alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, DPP‑4 inhibitors, GLP‑1 analogues, and biguanides) are organized under the overarching category of diabetes medications, with combination products and specific contraindications guiding safe and effective patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 1 -- Community 38
content: The therapeutic category “drugs used in diabetes” comprises insulin‑based products (insulins and analogues) and non‑insulin glucose‑lowering agents. Within the insulin family, long‑acting injectable analogues such as insulin glargine, insulin degludec and combination formulations that pair a basal insulin with a rapid‑acting component (e.g., beef insulin and human insulin) are classified as long‑acting or intermediate/long‑acting plus fast‑acting insulins. Fixed‑dose combinations that couple a basal insulin with a glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogue—insulin degludec / liraglutide and insulin glargine / lixisenatide—provide complementary glycaemic control. The GLP‑1 analogue class includes exenatid, liraglutide and lixisenatide. Non‑insulin agents include alpha‑glucosidase inhibitors, dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitors (e.g., alogliptin, sitagliptin) and biguanides (e.g., fenformin/metformin). Sitagliptin is incorporated in a fixed‑dose tablet with the statin simvastatin; simvastatin is contraindicated when co‑administered with the antiretroviral nelfinavir mesylate. This taxonomy links basal long‑acting insulin analogues, oral biguanides, DPP‑4 inhibitors and GLP‑1 analogues, highlighting their distinct mechanisms, common combination products, and important drug‑interaction considerations in diabetes management.

title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 772
content: Glucagon-like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues are a class of incretin‑based medications that include both exenatid and lixisenatide. Exenatid, as a member of the GLP‑1 analogue family, acts on the GLP‑1 receptor to enhance glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, suppress glucagon release, and slow gastric emptying. Lixisenatide similarly belongs to the GLP‑1 analogue class and shares these pharmacologic effects. In addition to being an individual GLP‑1 analogue, lixisenatide is incorporated into the fixed‑dose combination product insulin glargine/lixisenatide, which pairs basal insulin glargine with the GLP‑1 receptor agonist to provide combined basal insulin and incretin therapy for improved glycemic control in patients with type 2 diabetes. This integration underscores the therapeutic strategy of using GLP‑1 analogues both as standalone agents and as components of combination regimens to optimize blood glucose management.

content: DRUGS USED IN DIABETES is a broad therapeutic category that encompasses insulins and analogues. Within the insulins and analogues subclass, the formulation described as Insulins and analogues for injection used in Diabetes, intermediate- or long-acting combined with fast-acting is a further specialized group. This group consists of injectable insulin preparations that combine intermediate‑ or long‑acting basal insulin with rapid‑acting insulin to achieve both steady background glucose control and prompt post‑prandial reduction. title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 3 -- Community 1498
content: Centrally acting antiobesity products constitute a pharmacologic class that promotes weight loss by influencing central nervous system pathways involved in appetite and energy balance. This class includes the combination therapy bupropion / naltrexone, the melanocortin‑4 receptor agonist setmelanotide, and the serotonin‑norepinephrine reuptake inhibitor sibutramine. Bupropion / naltrexone works by modulating reward and satiety circuits, setmelanotide targets genetic forms of obesity through melanocortin signaling, and sibutramine reduces appetite by increasing synaptic concentrations of serotonin and norepinephrine. Together, these centrally acting agents illustrate the diverse mechanisms by which central pathways can be leveraged to treat obesity, highlighting the importance of individualized therapeutic selection based on efficacy, safety profile, and underlying patient characteristics.

content: Glucagon‑like peptide‑1 (GLP‑1) analogues comprise several agents, including exenatid, liraglutide, and lixisenatide. These GLP‑1 analogues are often combined with basal insulins to enhance glycaemic control in patients with diabetes. The fixed‑ratio combination product insulin degludec / liraglutide incorporates liraglutide, while the combination insulin glargine / lixisenatide contains lixisenatide. By pairing GLP‑1 receptor agonists such as liraglutide or lixisenatide with long‑acting insulins, clinicians can exploit complementary mechanisms—insulin’s basal glucose‑lowering effect and GLP‑1 analogues’ glucose‑dependent insulin secretion, delayed gastric emptying, and appetite suppression—to achieve more effective and convenient diabetes management.

title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 955
content: Nelfinavir mesylate is contraindicated with simvastatin, indicating that these two agents should not be used together because of a potential drug interaction. Simvastatin is one of the active ingredients in the fixed‑dose combination product simvastatin / sitagliptin, which therefore contains both a cholesterol‑lowering statin and a glucose‑lowering DPP‑4 inhibitor. Alogliptin is identified as a member of the dipeptidyl peptidase‑4 (DPP‑4) inhibitor class that is used to lower blood glucose, and sitagliptin is likewise a DPP‑4 inhibitor. Because sitagliptin is a component of the simvastatin / sitagliptin combination, the combination product is considered a member of the DPP‑4 inhibitor class as well. This interrelationship highlights the need to avoid co‑administration of nelfinavir mesylate with simvastatin while recognizing that the simvastatin / sitagliptin product simultaneously addresses dyslipidemia and hyperglycemia through its constituent agents.
